rationale: This is a canonical example of the Charette asymmetric cyclopropanation of a simple allylic alcohol (allyl alcohol) to form an optically active cyclopropylmethanol. It uses the standard Simmons-Smith reagents (Et2Zn, CH2I2) in combination with a chiral dioxaborolane ligand derived from tartaric acid, which is characteristic of the Charette method. The substrate is simple, acyclic, and avoids complex motifs.
collision_avoidance_note: The reaction specifically involves an allylic alcohol, which directs the cyclopropanation and is key to the Charette method. This avoids collision with general Simmons-Smith cyclopropanations of unfunctionalized alkenes. The use of a specific chiral ligand (dioxaborolane) and zinc-based reagents distinguishes it from other asymmetric cyclopropanation methods (e.g., those using copper or rhodium catalysts with diazo compounds).
